1:
"And the men of Kirjathjearim came, and fetched up the ark of the LORD, and brought it into the house of Abinadab in the hill, and sanctified Eleazar his son to keep the ark of the LORD." The very brief verse notes the ark brought to the house of Abinadab, his son set to keep it. The grammatical voices note the keeping. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the men of Kirjathjearim came, and fetched up the ark... and brought it into the house of Abinadab in the hill, and sanctified Eleazar his son to keep the ark" — the ark was lodged in Abinadab's house and a keeper appointed; the and the men of Kirjathjearim came, and fetched up the ark of the LORD, and brought it into the house of Abinadab in the hill (the ark lodged); the and sanctified Eleazar his son to keep the ark of the LORD (the keeper); the figure (the ark given a resting place and a keeper, the holy thing reverently kept; the truth that the ark was lodged in Abinadab's house with a keeper, 2 Samuel 6:3; the figure of the holy presence reverently housed among God's people; the ark at Kirjathjearim; the keeper sanctified, vv.6:21). The verse holds together the keeping.

The verse displays the ark kept in Abinadab's house.

The verse holds together the lament.

3:
"And Samuel spake unto all the house of Israel, saying, If ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods and Ashtaroth from among you, and prepare your hearts unto the LORD, and serve him only: and he will deliver you out of the hand of the Philistines." The witnesses note Samuel's call to Israel to return to the LORD with all their hearts, put away their idols, and serve him only — the crux of true repentance, the whole-hearted turning from idols to the exclusive service of God. On "If ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods... and serve him only," interpreters expound the call. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the marks of true repentance — "if ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods and Ashtaroth from among you, and prepare your hearts unto the LORD, and serve him only: and he will deliver you": Samuel sets before Israel the whole nature of genuine returning to God; the witnesses note that without this whole-hearted turning the lament of twenty years would avail nothing (the heart wholly returned; the idols wholly forsaken; the service wholly God's); the lesson — that true repentance is the turning of the whole heart to God, forsaking all idols and serving him alone, "rend your heart, and not your garments, and turn unto the LORD your God," Joel 2:13; the truth that to return to God is to put away every idol and cleave to him only, "wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate... and I will receive you," 2 Corinthians 6:17; the figure — the whole-hearted repentance that forsakes idols and serves God only, fulfilled in those who turn to God from idols to serve the living God, "ye turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God," 1 Thessalonians 1:9. The figure of true repentance, the whole-hearted turning from idols to the exclusive service of God.

On "And Samuel spake unto all the house of Israel, saying, If ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods and Ashtaroth from among you," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the forsaking of idols. Keil and Ellicott note Samuel calling Israel to return with all the heart and put away their idols, the forsaking of every false god as the proof of true returning (the forsaking of idols, Joel 2:13) — the call (put away the strange gods).

On "and prepare your hearts unto the LORD, and serve him only: and he will deliver you out of the hand of the Philistines," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the whole-hearted service. Calvin and Gill note Samuel requiring the heart prepared and fixed on God and his exclusive service, true repentance forsaking every rival and yielding undivided devotion, with deliverance promised to such returning (the exclusive service, 2 Corinthians 6:17; 1 Thessalonians 1:9) — the figure (the whole-hearted repentance that forsakes idols and serves God only); the providence (the call, Israel putting away Baalim and Ashtaroth to follow, vv.4). The verse displays Samuel's call to whole-hearted repentance.

The witnesses agree the substance: Samuel sets before Israel the whole nature of genuine returning to God (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of true repentance: return unto the LORD with all your hearts... and serve him only — the anatomy of true repentance, not mere lament but returning to the LORD with all the heart, proved by three marks: putting away the strange gods (forsaking all idols), preparing the heart unto the LORD (fixing the soul on God), and serving him only (undivided devotion), so that true repentance is whole-hearted, forsakes every rival, sets the heart on God, and yields him entire service, with deliverance attached, the turning of the whole heart to God and forsaking all idols (Joel 2:13), coming out and being separate to cleave to him only (2 Corinthians 6:17), turning to God from idols to serve the living God (1 Thessalonians 1:9). The verse displays the repentance crux. Samuel's call to whole-hearted repentance displays the crux: If ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods... and serve him only (Joel 2:13; 2 Corinthians 6:17; 1 Thessalonians 1:9) — true repentance, the whole-hearted turning from idols to the exclusive service of God, the forsaking of every rival and the fixing of the heart on God, with the promise of deliverance to those who wholly return. The verse displays Israel putting away Baalim and Ashtaroth and serving the LORD only (vv.4).

9:
"And Samuel took a sucking lamb, and offered it for a burnt offering wholly unto the LORD: and Samuel cried unto the LORD for Israel; and the LORD heard him." The witnesses note Samuel offering a sucking lamb wholly to the LORD and crying for Israel, and the LORD heard him — the crux of the prevailing prayer joined to sacrifice, the intercession that, resting on the offering, is heard and answered of God. On "Samuel cried unto the LORD for Israel; and the LORD heard him," interpreters expound the prayer. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the prayer that prevails through sacrifice — "and Samuel took a sucking lamb, and offered it for a burnt offering wholly unto the LORD: and Samuel cried unto the LORD for Israel; and the LORD heard him": Samuel joins sacrifice to intercession, and God answers; the witnesses note that the burnt offering, wholly consumed, signified the entire devotion of the people to God and pointed to the one all-sufficient sacrifice, so that the prevailing of Samuel's prayer was grounded not in his own worthiness but in the offering on which it leaned, the type of him through whom alone all prayer is heard (the prayer joined to sacrifice; the offering wholly given; the intercession heard for the sacrifice's sake); the lesson — that prayer prevails with God when it rests upon sacrifice and is offered in faith, "the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much," James 5:16; the truth that our prayers are accepted not for themselves but through the one offering of Christ, "by whom also we have access by faith into this grace," Romans 5:2; the figure — the intercession that prevails through the sacrifice, fulfilled in the great Intercessor who ever lives to make intercession on the ground of his own offering, "he is able also to save them to the uttermost... seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them," Hebrews 7:25. The figure of the prevailing prayer joined to sacrifice, the intercession that, resting on the offering, is heard and answered of God.

12:
"Then Samuel took a stone, and set it between Mizpeh and Shen, and called the name of it Ebenezer, saying, Hitherto hath the LORD helped us." The witnesses note Samuel setting up a stone called Ebenezer, "hitherto hath the LORD helped us" — the crux of the memorial of God's help, the thankful remembrance that traces every past deliverance to God and trusts him for the future. On "called the name of it Ebenezer, saying, Hitherto hath the LORD helped us," interpreters expound the memorial. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the stone of remembered help — "then Samuel took a stone, and set it between Mizpeh and Shen, and called the name of it Ebenezer, saying, Hitherto hath the LORD helped us": Samuel raises a lasting memorial of the LORD's help in the day of victory; the witnesses note the duty of erecting such memorials, that God's people may not forget his benefits but keep them in lasting remembrance (the stone a witness of God's help; the past mercy owned; the future trust grounded on it); the lesson — that we are to remember and record the help of God, raising memorials of his mercy lest we forget, "forget not all his benefits," Psalm 103:2; the truth that the remembrance of past help is the ground of present and future confidence, "thou hast been my help; leave me not... O God of my salvation," Psalm 27:9; the figure — the thankful memorial that owns God's past help and trusts his future faithfulness, fulfilled in the assurance that he who has helped will help still, "being confident of this very thing, that he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it," Philippians 1:6. The figure of the memorial of God's help, the thankful remembrance that traces every past deliverance to God and trusts him for the future.

Calvin and Gill note the grateful confession that owns every deliverance to that hour as God's, and, in the word "hitherto," trusts that the God who had helped thus far would help still, the past mercy the ground of future trust (the help owned and trusted, Psalm 27:9; Philippians 1:6) — the figure (the thankful memorial that owns God's past help and trusts his future faithfulness); the providence (the memorial, the Philistines subdued all the days of Samuel to follow, vv.13).
